coredata.dc:title: Al3+-triggered aggregation-induced fluorescence enhanced carbon dots sensor for the recognition of lysine enantiomers in dairy products
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Kaixin
Ni, Wanqing
Wang, Chenzhao
Su, Kaihua
Yin, Xiangyu
Song, Xuewei
Liu, Min
Sun, Guoying
Wei, Shanshan
coredata.dc:description: L-Lysine (L-Lys) as an essential amino acid is abundant in nutrient-rich dairy products. However, patients with glutaric aciduria type I are required to follow a lysine-restricted diet. Therefore, it is very essential to selectively identify L-Lys for food safety and disease treatment. Herein, chiral nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CN-CDs) were prepared by a single-step hydrothermal procedure for the high specific discrimination of Lys enantiomers. The fluorescence of CN-CDs could be significantly enhanced through Al3+-triggered aggregation-induced emission (AIE). And the fluorescence of CN-CDs-Al3+ system could be effectively quenched by L-Lys through static quenching and inner filter effect, thus the obtained CN-CDs-Al3+ sensor displayed excellent chiral recognition ability for lysine enantiomers. An outstanding linear correlation was established between the fluorescence intensity of CN-CDs-Al3+ system and L-Lys concentration with a low detection limit (LOD) of 0.42 µM. Gaussian calculations revealed that the selectivity of CN-CDs-Al3+ for lysine enantiomers was due to the stereoselectivity and different hydrogen bond interactions between the CN-CDs-Al3+ and lysine. Furthermore, the CDs-based fluorescence sensor was utilized for the quantitative detection of L-Lys in dairy products, with a spiked recovery rate from 94.83 % to 118.25 %. Significantly, the portable detection of L-Lys was achieved by combining visual CDs-based gel sensors with a smartphone chromaticity analysis APP. Therefore, the CN-CDs-based sensor demonstrated a promising application in the field of smart devices-supported portable detection for the management of lysine metabolic diseases.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Sensor
Lysine enantiomer
Dairy product
Aggregation-induced emission

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of l-lys in dairy product samples
Smartphone application for the detection of Al3+ and l-lys

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and HR-TEM image, (b) particle size distribution, (c-d) AFM images, (e) Raman spectrum and (f) XRD pattern of the CN-CDs.
(a) FT-IR spectrum and (b) full-survey XPS of CN-CDs. High-resolution XPS of C 1 s (c), N 1 s (d) and O 1 s (e).

(a) Spectra of excitation, emission, and absorption for CN-CDs. Insert: pictures of CN-CDs under sunlight (left) and UV lamp (right). (b) Excitation dependent fluorescence spectra of CN-CDs (c) Circular dichroism of CN-CDs. (d) CIE spectra of CN-CDs.
Fluorescence intensity of CN-CDs under the conditions of (a) H2O2, (b) NaCl, (c) continuous illumination under ultraviolet lamp for 50 min, (d) temperature, (e) pH, and (f) different storage times.

The selectivity of (a) CN-CDs and (b) CN-CDs-Al3+ system, (c) the fluorescence variation of CN-CDs when exposed to Al3+, (d) the fluorescence of CN-CDs-Al3+ system with varying concentrations of L-Lys. (e) the fluorescence of CN-CDs-Al3+ system with varying concentrations of L-Lys and D-Lys (Image of different concentrations of L-Lys and D-Lys under 365 nm ultraviolet light) and (f) Linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and ee.

(a) UV absorption spectra, (b) TEM of CN-CDs-Al3+, (c) XPS of CN-CDs + Al3+, (d) Zeta potential, (e) fluorescence lifetime of CN-CDs and the addition of Al3+ and L/D-Lys, (f) the Ksv values of CN-CDs-Al3+ and L-Lys at different temperatures, (g) HOMOs and LUMOs for CN-CDs-Al3+ and L-Lys, possible interactions between CN-CDs-Al3+ and L-Lys (h) or D-Lys (i).

(a) CN-CDs gel microsphere in the box with a 365 nm UV lamp. (b) Schematic drawing for the detection of Al3+ or L-Lys using a smartphone. Photographs of CN-CDs-impregnated gel microspheres in different concentrations of (c) Al3+ and (d) L-Lys. (e) Linear relationship between green channel value with Al3+ concentrations. (f) Linear relationship between R/G value with L-Lys concentrations. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Images of HeLa cells cultured with CN-CDs (300 μg/mL), CN-CDs + Al3+ (100 μM), CN-CDs-Al3+ + L-Lys (100 μM) and CN-CDs-Al3+ + D-Lys (100 μM).
The preparation of CN-CDs and the principle and application for lysine enantiomers detection.
Determination of L-Lys in milk products.

row.entry:
Inital L-Lys amount determined by CN-CDs
(μmol/L)

head.ce:title: Al3+-triggered aggregation-induced fluorescence enhanced carbon dots sensor for the recognition of lysine enantiomers in dairy products

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Life Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: L-Lysine (L-Lys) as an essential amino acid is abundant in nutrient-rich dairy products. However, patients with glutaric aciduria type I are required to follow a lysine-restricted diet. Therefore, it is very essential to selectively identify L-Lys for food safety and disease treatment. Herein, chiral nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CN-CDs) were prepared by a single-step hydrothermal procedure for the high specific discrimination of Lys enantiomers. The fluorescence of CN-CDs could be significantly enhanced through Al3+-triggered aggregation-induced emission (AIE). And the fluorescence of CN-CDs-Al3+ system could be effectively quenched by L-Lys through static quenching and inner filter effect, thus the obtained CN-CDs-Al3+ sensor displayed excellent chiral recognition ability for lysine enantiomers. An outstanding linear correlation was established between the fluorescence intensity of CN-CDs-Al3+ system and L-Lys concentration with a low detection limit (LOD) of 0.42 µM. Gaussian calculations revealed that the selectivity of CN-CDs-Al3+ for lysine enantiomers was due to the stereoselectivity and different hydrogen bond interactions between the CN-CDs-Al3+ and lysine. Furthermore, the CDs-based fluorescence sensor was utilized for the quantitative detection of L-Lys in dairy products, with a spiked recovery rate from 94.83 % to 118.25 %. Significantly, the portable detection of L-Lys was achieved by combining visual CDs-based gel sensors with a smartphone chromaticity analysis APP. Therefore, the CN-CDs-based sensor demonstrated a promising application in the field of smart devices-supported portable detection for the management of lysine metabolic diseases.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chirality holds a crucial role in the emergence and development of life, and it is an inherent characteristic of most bioactive substances such as amino acids, proteins, and DNA [1,2]. Among them, amino acids with unique chiral centers determine the three-dimensional structure of proteins, which influences enzyme catalytic activity [3]. They naturally exist in two forms of L and D enantiomers [2]. Although chiral enantiomers exhibit comparable chemical architectures, they exhibited different activities in various physiological processes and even opposite effects in certain cases [4]. L-lysine (L-Lys) is a vital amino acid [4] that serves as an energy source for the repair of neurons and the maintenance of regular physiological functions [5], whereas D-lysine (D-Lys) has been demonstrated to be cytotoxic [2]. Nevertheless, irregular L-Lys metabolism resulted from a deficiency in enzymes can trigger a range of neurometabolic disorders such as hyperlysinemia, glutaric aciduria type I and pyridoxine-dependent epilepsy [6]. Notably, common dairy products contain high level of L-Lys, which is not suitable for consumption by individuals with lysine metabolic disorders [7]. Thus, establishing efficient strategies for the detection of lysine enantiomers is of great significance in food safety and disease treatment [8].

At present, many methods are widely applied to the detection of lysine enantiomers including electrochemical method [9], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [10], synchrotron X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) [11] and laser desorption/ionization mass spectrometry (LDI-MS) [12]. However, many of them suffer from obvious shortcomings, such as high equipment costs, complex sample pre-treatment, time-consuming procedures, and excessive consumption of toxic organic solvents. Compared with conventional methods, fluorescent probes demonstrated distinctive advantages due to their simple operation, excellent selectivity, and high sensitivity [1]. Many fluorescent probes based on organic fluorescent dyes, metal–organic frameworks (MOFs), gold nanoclusters (NCs), and chromium quantum dots(CQDs) have been developed for detecting food additives, environmental organic pollutants, and disease biomarkers [13]. However, these probes commonly face all kinds of limitations: MOF probes have a complex synthesis process and poor stability, NCs are costly to prepare and have low quantum yield, and CQDs and organic dye probes exhibit biotoxicity, which significantly restricts their practical application.

In recent years, CDs have garnered extensive interests owing to their superior biocompatibility [1], low cost and simple preparation [2].They are applied in the fields of fluorescent sensor, cell imaging [2], loading drugs delivery [14] and photoelectric devices [15]. Especially, CDs-based fluorescence sensors display great application prospects in the food safety field for detecting ions, amino acids and enzyme [1,16]. For example, Raji et al. prepared nitrogen-doped CDs from jackfruit seeds that exhibited a high quantum yield of 17.91 % and successfully detected Au3+ in an aqueous medium [17]. Ni et al. synthesized nitrogen and boron co-doped CDs to detect alkaline phosphatase on the basis of the specific reaction between boric acid and cis-diols, which could cause particle aggregation and fluorescence quenching [18]. Although the development of CDs-based fluorescent sensors offers a portable and efficient tool for the detection of bioactive molecules, the exploration of CDs-based fluorescence sensors for the chiral recognition of amino acids has rarely been reported [19].Gao and colleagues prepared chiral CDs using citric acid and L-aspartic acid, which could rapidly and sensitively recognize lysine enantiomers by fluorescence switching in the presence of Sn2+ [20]. Zhang and colleagues developed zinc-doped CDs for detecting Fe2+ and lysine enantiomers in aqueous solution through a fluorescence change process of “on-off-on” [21]. Sharma et al. synthesized blue-light emitting CDs from Nigella sativa seeds, and the fluorescence intensity of these CDs could be quenched by tetracycline and subsequently enhanced by L-Lys through aggregation-induced emission [22]. Although the previously mentioned CDs can selectively and quantitatively detect L-Lys, the fabrication of fluorescent CD sensors for the selective, sensitive, and accurate recognition of amino acid enantiomers remains as a substantial challenge.

Herein, chiral nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CN-CDs) were prepared using metformin hydrochloride and 2,5-dihydroxypbenzoic acid as precursors through a simple one-step hydrothermal process for the specific recognition of lysine enantiomers (scheme 1 ). The prepared CN-CDs exhibited yellow fluorescence emission at 520 nm under 350 nm excitation, which could be effectively enhanced through Al3+-triggered aggregation-induced effect by forming Al-O bonds between Al3+ and CN-CDs. Interestingly, the CN-CDs-Al3+ system could effectively facilitate the discrimination of L/D-Lys with high selectivity due to the different steric hindrance and free energy between the Lys enantiomers and the CN-CDs-Al3+ system according to gaussian calculations. The combination of CDs-loaded gel microspheres and smartphones promoted portable and visual identification of L-Lys. More importantly, the CN-CDs-based fluorescent sensor had been successfully applied to distinguish Lys enantiomers in dairy products and living cells owing to its superior optical properties and outstanding biocompatibility, broadening the potential application of CDs in food and biological sensing areas.

First, 0.1 g of metformin hydrochloride and an equal amount of DHTA were dissolved in 15 mL of pure deionized water. The prepared mixture was poured into a Teflon reactor and maintained at 180 ℃ for 6 h. Upon reaching room temperature, the resultant mixture was spun at 10,000 rpm for 8 min in a centrifuge and then passed through a filter with the pore diameter of 0.22 μm. The brown liquid was subjected to a 24-h dialysis process using a dialysis membrane with a molecular weight cut-off of 1000 Da to obtain a purified solution of CN-CDs.

To investigate the stability of CN-CDs across various environments, the solution of CN-CDs at 0.1 g/mL was employed for testing. The ionic strength stability performance of CN-CDs towards NaCl was evaluated by analyzing the fluorescence spectra of the CN-CDs solution after various concentrations of NaCl were added. The photostability of CN-CDs against photobleaching was tested by assessing their fluorescence intensity under 365 nm UV illumination over a period of 0 to 50 min. To evaluate its thermal stability, 3 mL of CN-CDs solution (0.2 mg/mL) was subjected in a constant temperature water bath at different temperatures for a duration of 10 min. Finally, the fluorescence intensity of the CN-CDs aqueous solutions with a pH range of 1.0 to 14.0 was examined.

The CDs solution was diluted to the concentration of 10 mg/mL for detecting Al3+. Diverse concentrations of Al3+ solution were added into the CDs solutions, and the fluorescence changes of CN-CDs were recorded. A series of concentrations of L/D-Lys solution were blended into the CN-CDs-Al3+ system at ambient temperature, then 3 mL of this blend was poured into a quartz cuvette for fluorescence analysis. After reaching equilibrium, the fluorescence intensity of the solutions was recorded at the optimal excitation wavelength (λex = 350 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of l-lys in dairy product samples
ce:section.ce:para: The capability of CN-CDs to detect L-Lys in actual food samples was assessed. Five dairy products with different L-Lys concentrations were selected as representative samples to evaluate the sensor's potential for real samples (two powdered milk varieties (Maxigenes and Mengniu Women's Milk Powder) and three liquid milk products (Deluxe Milk, Fresh Milk, and Yogurt)). For the solid samples, 0.2 g of dairy product was diluted with 10 mL of deionized water. For liquid samples, 100 μL of the milk was diluted to 10 mL. The resulting raw samples were then subjected to a 5-minute ultrasonic treatment for subsequent analysis.

The impact of CN-CDs on the viability of HeLa cells were assessed utilizing the CCK-8 method. HeLa cells were diluted with DMEM supplemented with 1 % double antibodies and 10 % fetal bovine serum, then seeded into a 96-well plate, and incubated overnight at 37 °C in a 5 % CO2 environment. After the mixed solution was removed, 100 µL of DMEM with varying concentrations of CN-CDs (0–800 µg/mL) was infused into the wells. Following a 24-h incubation period, the medium containing CN-CDs was discarded, and the cells were rinsed twice with PBS buffer. Subsequently, 100 μL of CCK-8 solution was added to the blank group, cell group, and test substance group, followed by an additional 4 h incubation. The optical density at 450 nm for each well was recorded with a microplate reader (BioTek, USA) to determine cell activity. Non-treated cells were chosen as control.

The cellular imaging capability of CN-CDs was evaluated using HeLa cells. Cells were plated in a 6-well dish, and the culture conditions were the same as mentioned above. Subsequently, the cells were incubated in culture medium containing CN-CDs at a final concentration of 300 μM for another 4 h. To investigate the changes of intracellular fluorescence, Al3+ solution (100 μM) and lysine solution (100 μM) were added into cells in glass culture dish. After the cells were further kept for 1 h, fluorescence imaging of the cells was conducted using an inverted fluorescence microscope.

To optimize the optical performance of CN-CDs, the optimal preparation conditions for CDs were investigated. The optimal fluorescence intensity was obtained when the mass ratio of DHTA and metformin hydrochloride was 1:1, reaction temperature and reaction time were 180 ℃ and 6 h, respectively (Fig. S1a-S1c). The shape and size range of the CN-CDs were examined through TEM and atomic force microscope (AFM). As displayed in Fig. 1 a, the CN-CDs were nearly spherical particles and had a homogenous size distribution with the average diameter of 1.08 ± 0.05 nm (Fig. 1b). The HR-TEM image of CN-CDs revealed a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm (the inset of Fig. 1a), corresponding to the graphene (100) crystal plane [23]. The AFM image of CN-CDs showed an average particle height of 1.00 nm (Fig. 1c and d), which was close to the TEM analysis result. The Raman spectroscopy data showed two peaks at 1390 cm−1 and 1586 cm−1 (Fig. 1e), which were indicative of the typical D and G bands in carbonaceous materials, signifying the presence of disordered sp3 carbon and graphitic sp2 carbon [24,25]. Furthermore, a strong absorption peak at approximately 26.5° was observed in the XRD pattern (Fig. 1f), corresponding to the (002) plane of graphitic structure [26].

The information of the functional groups on the surface of CN-CDs was obtained through the FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2 a). The broad band observed between 3600 cm−1 and 3100 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibrations of N-H and O-H bonds [27].The absorption band at 1632 cm−1 and 1427 cm−1 corresponded to the C=O and the asymmetric stretching vibration of the carboxylate anions, indicating the existence of –COOH groups [16]. The characteristic peaks at 1512 cm−1 and 1499 cm−1 were derived from stretching vibrations of the aromatic C=C and C-C bonds [28,29], respectively. Moreover, the peak at 1247 cm−1 was attributed to C-N bonds [1]. The element composition and the chemical state of the CN-CDs were determined by XPS spectra. The full-range XPS spectrum of CN-CDs was displayed in Fig. 2b, and the signals of C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, and Cl 2p were observed at 284.65 eV, 399.8 eV and 531.85 eV, respectively. The C1s spectrum was deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.5 eV (C=C/C-C), 285.7 eV (C-O/C-N), and 288.5 eV (O-C=O), respectively [21] (Fig. 2c). The N 1 s spectrum was characterized by the presence of two fitting peaks at 399.9 eV and 401.5 eV (Fig. 2d), which could arise from the pyrrolic and amino N. O 1 s spectrum was separated into two peaks of 531.7 eV(C=O) and 532.9 eV(C-OH/C-O-C) [2] (Fig. 2e). These analysis data revealed that the surface layer of CN-CDs was abundant in variety of hydrophilic functional groups including hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups, which endowed CN-CDs with outstanding water compatibility and facilitated their better application in subsequent analyses.

The fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra were utilized to examine the optical characteristics of CN-CDs. The CN-CDs solution was colorless in sunlight and radiated a bright yellow fluorescence when exposed to 365 nm ultraviolet light (Fig. 3 a). These CDs exhibited two absorption peaks in the visible light region at 212 nm and 240–250 nm, corresponding to the π → π* transitions of C=C bonds in the benzene ring [1].The wide absorption band between 300 nm and 400 nm corresponded to the n → π* transition of C=O bond [30]. To examine the fluorescence characteristic of the CN-CDs, their emission spectra were collected at a variety of excitation wavelengths. The emission of CN-CDs remained at 520 nm as the excitation wavelength increased (Fig. 3b), which indicated that these CN-CDs possessed an excitation-independent emission property owing to the relatively uniform surface defects and particle size of CN-CDs [31]. Furthermore, CN-CDs exhibited the strongest emission peak at 520 nm under an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. The photophysical properties of CN-CDs were investigated using circular dichroism spectroscopy. As illustrated in Fig. 3c, a stronger negative signal at 206 nm and a weaker positive signal at 240 nm appeared, indicating two chiral centers existed in CN-CDs. Moreover, the CN-CDs exhibited a fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of 20.99 % measured against water as reference, surpassing most reported CDs with similar structures (Table S1), which was conducive to enhancing detection sensitivity and accuracy (Fig. S2) [16]. Finally, the fluorescence results matched the CIE coordinates (0.27, 0.52) (Fig. 3d), confirming the yellow-green emission of CN-CDs. The findings indicated that CN-CDs possessed superior optical properties and high quantum yield, which were beneficial for their further application in the analysis and sensing of intricate biological specimens.

To improve the sensitivity of the detection system, the stability of CN-CDs was evaluated in response to various factors, including H2O2, NaCl, temperature, UV irradiation, and pH changes. When CN-CDs were added into different concentrations of H2O2 solution, the fluorescence intensity remained basically unchanged (Fig. 4 a), indicating that CN-CDs had excellent antioxidant properties. The fluorescence fluctuations of CN-CDs were negligible even NaCl concentrations were as high as 1.0 M (Fig. 4b), suggesting that these CDs exhibited good stability in highly saline solutions. And it did not obviously diminish following 50 min of continuous exposure to a UV lamp at 365 nm (Fig. 4c), which demonstrated that UV irradiation had little impact on the fluorescence properties of CN-CDs. The fluorescence intensity of CN-CDs maintained stable when the temperature increased from 0 ℃ to 100 ℃ (Fig. 4d). Subsequently, the stability of CDs in different acidic and alkaline environments was explored (Fig. 4e). The fluorescent signal intensity of the CN-CDs remained relatively constant within the pH range of 3.0 to 12.0. Nevertheless, the fluorescence of CN-CDs intensified at pH levels below 2.0 and diminished at pH level above 13.0, attributable to the protonation and deprotonation process of the carboxyl and amine groups on the CN-CDs’ surface [16]. The fluorescence intensity of CN-CDs did not exhibit obvious changes after 50 days of storage at room temperature (Fig. 4f). These results confirmed the excellent photostability of the CN-CDs, which enhanced the analytical and sensory capabilities of CDs in real samples and living cells.

Excessive Al3+ may lead to many diseases including Alzheimer’s disease, Parkinson’s disease, glucose intolerance and cardiac arrest [6]. Thus, it is critical to achieve the efficient and selective detection of Al3+.The response of the fluorescence intensity of CN-CDs to Al3+ over time was investigated, and the optimal detection conditions were determined: room temperature (Fig. 4d), pH = 7.0 (Fig. 4e), and an incubation time of 3 min (Fig. S3). To assess the sensor's resistance to interference, metal ions including biologically rich K+, Na+, Ca2+ and Zn2+, transition metal ions Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Cr3+, and rare earth ions La3+, Ce3+, and Pr3+ were selected. As presented in Fig. 5 a, the addition of other ions did not significantly alter the fluorescence intensity of CN-CDs nor interfere with the Al3+-induced fluorescence enhancement, demonstrating their excellent anti-interference capability. Additionally, Fe3+ induced a certain degree of fluorescence quenching of CN-CDs. It was found that L-Ascorbic acid (L-AA) as a masking agent could effectively eliminate the false-negative results induced by Fe3+ (Fig. S4). Thereafter, the fluorescence of CN-CDs was measured in varying concentrations of Al3+. The fluorescence intensity of CN-CDs was observed to gradually increase with the increased concentration of Al3+ (Fig. 5c). Importantly, a superior linear correlation between the (F-F0)/F0 value (F0 and F denoted the fluorescence intensity of CN-CDs without and with the addition of Al3+, respectively) and Al3+ concentration (0–100 μM) was obtained (Fig. S5a). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.34 μM according to the formula 3σ/K, where σ denoted the standard deviation of the blank samples and K was the slope of the calibration curve. Compared with other reported methods based on CDs, the proposed sensing system possessed wider linear range and lower detection limit (Table S2). These results demonstrated that the CN-CDs could serve as a distinctive and sensitive fluorescent sensor for the detection of Al3+.

Lysine is an essential amino acid for maintaining human life activities and is found in various milk products [27]. However, these dairy products also contain different types of other amino acids. To verify the selectivity of CN-CDs to lysine enantiomers, the influence of other amino acids on the fluorescence of CDs had to be investigated including D/L-Asp, D/L-Thr, D/L-Glu, L-Ala, L-Cys, L-Gln, L-Leu, L-Met, PGA, L-Pro, L-Try, L-Tyr and L-Val. As presented in Fig. 5b, the addition of other amino acids only altered slightly the fluorescence intensity of CN-CDs-Al3+ and did not interfere with the L-Lys-induced quenching effect, which could be attributed to the formation of hydrogen bonds between the distinctive ((CH2)4NH3 +) side chain of L-Lys and the carboxyl groups (COO–) of CN-CDs [22]. Furthermore, optimization experiments and kinetic studies revealed that CN-CDs + Al3+ system exhibited outstanding selectivity toward L-Lys at pH = 7.0, room temperature and 6 min incubation (Fig. S6 a-c). As the concentration of L-Lys rose from 0 μM to 200 μM, the fluorescence intensity of the CN-CDs-Al3+ complex decreased progressively, with a LOD of 0.42 μM (Fig. 5d and Fig. S5b). Upon the addition of varying concentrations of D-Lys, the fluorescence of CN-CDs-Al3+ gradually decreased with a linear correlation (R2 = 0.830) and a LOD of 1.66 μM (Fig. S7a, b). These results demonstrate that L-Lys significantly quenched CN-CDs-Al3+ fluorescence, while D-Lys caused only minor reduction, indicating the system's remarkable chiral recognition capability for L/D-Lys.

The enantiomeric excess (ee) is defined as the absolute difference in concentrations between the two enantiomers divided by the sum of their concentrations, providing a quantitative measure of chiral purity. Since the CN-CDs exhibited different fluorescence response for lysine enantiomers, it could be employed to quantitatively detect them through an ee approach with the following calculation formula [32].
(1)
ee =
( [ L ] - [ D ] )
L
+
[ D ]
∗ 100 %

The CN-CDs-Al3+ system was utilized for assessing various mixed solutions of L-Lys and D-Lys with different ee values. Fig. 5e illustrated that the system's fluorescence intensity progressively diminished with the rising concentration of L-Lys. Fluorescence spectral analysis demonstrated a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of the mixture and the measured ee values across the linear range of −100 % to 100 %(R2 = 0.996) (Fig. 5f). The significantly different slopes for the two lysine enantiomers displayed that the obtained CN-CDs had the capability to not only distinguish the lysine chirality but also predict the concentrations of lysine enantiomers in their respective mixtures. This property endowed the CN-CDs-Al3+ system with significant potential application in the fields of food safety and health risk identification.

The possible mechanism of the interaction between CN-CDs and Al3+ was explored by UV–vis absorption spectrum, TEM, FT-IR spectroscopy, Zeta potential and the time-resolved fluorescence attenuation curve. Generally, the fluorescence enhancement mechanisms of CDs triggered by metal ions primarily encompassed aggregation-induced fluorescence enhancement (AIE) and metal-induced fluorescence enhancement (MFE) [33]. Upon the addition of Al3+, CN-CDs exhibited a new absorption peak at 205 nm(Fig. 6 a), signifying the formation of a novel complex [30]. Additionally, no sharp plasmon resonance peak was observed, indicating that the fluorescence enhancement was not due to MFE [33]. Moreover, the CN-CDs displayed significant aggregation in the presence of Al3+ as shown in TEM image (Fig. 6b), indicating that the fluorescence enhancement mechanism could be attributed to AIE. To examine the interaction of Al3+ with the CN-CDs surface groups, XPS and FT-IR spectrum was analyzed. As shown in the Al 2p XPS spectrum of CN-CDs-Al3+ (Fig. 6c), two fitted peaks at 74.50 eV and 75.10 eV were observed, which corresponded to the bonds of Al-O and Al-O-C [34], further indicating the formation of Al-O bonds between CN-CDs and Al3+. The stretching vibration of the phenolic hydroxyl group of CN-CDs-Al3+ at 3200 cm−1 was intensified, and the peaks of the carboxyl group at 1630 cm−1 and 1380 cm−1 significantly broadened and enhanced, further confirming the formation of Al-O bonds [35]. Moreover, the new peak at 620 cm−1 could be ascribed to the stretching vibration of the Al-O bonds [25] (Fig. S8). Thus, the fluorescence enhancement of the CN-CDs + Al3+ system might originate from the formation of Al-O bonds between Al3+ and CN-CDs, which restricted the rotation of the functional groups on the CN-CDs surface, thereby exhibiting the AIE phenomenon. Upon the addition of Al3+ to CN-CDs solution, the Zeta potential rapidly enhanced from −15.64 mV to 2.77 mV (Fig. 6d). The absolute potential value of the CN-CDs-Al3+ system was less than 10 mV, suggesting that aggregation might occur within the system [2,36]. Meanwhile, the fluorescence lifetime of CN-CDs + Al3+ system increased from 4.46 ns of CN-CDs to 7.09 ns (Fig. 6e), similar to the Al3+-induced fluorescence lifetime increase of AA-CDs [19]. The results indicated that the formation of Al-O bonds between CN-CDs and Al3+ restricted the vibration of functional groups and reduced nonradiative transitions, which in turn led to an increase in the fluorescence intensity and lifetime of CN-CDs. An obvious blue shift was observed owing to the π–π stacking interactions during aggregation, which altered the electronic environment of CDs and elevated the excited-state energy level [37,38].

Subsequently, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CN-CDs-Al3+ by L-Lys was investigated. The fluorescence lifetime of CN-CDs + Al3+ slightly changed from 7.09 ns to 7.03 ns after adding L-Lys, indicating static quenching might occur rather than dynamic quenching[1]. Subsequently, the Stern-Volmer equation (Eq ) was utilized to gain a deeper insight into the static quenching mechanism [16].
(2)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
sv
Q
= 1 +
K q
τ 0
Q

F0 and F represented the fluorescence intensity without and with L-Lys, respectively. [Q] referred to the concentration of the quencher. Ksv and Kq were the quenching constant and quenching rate constant from the Stern-Volmer equation, respectively. And τ0 represented the inherent fluorescence lifetime of CN-CDs-Al3+.

The quenching effect of L-Lys on CN-CDs-Al3+ at various temperatures was investigated [16]. The values of Ksv declined as the temperature increased (Ksv (20℃) ＞ Ksv (40℃) ＞ Ksv (60℃)) (Fig. 6f), indicating that the fluorescence reduction of CDs is due to static quenching. And the Kq was calculated to be 1.0 × 1017 L⋅mol−1⋅s−1, which surpassed the typical dynamic quenching constant of 2.0 × 1010 L⋅mol−1⋅s−1, indicating that the quenching process was likely static [1,39].

For further verification, the quenching process before and after the addition of lysine enantiomer was investigated. The UV–vis absorption of L-Lys at 200–400 nm overlapped obviously with the excitation spectrum of CN-CDs + Al3+ (Fig. S9), thus the fluorescence quenching could be relative to inner filter effect (IFE) [16]. In addition, the density functional theory (DFT) simulation was carried out using Gaussian 16 software and B3LYP/6-31G(d) method to further verify the static quenching process. The energy values of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of CN-CDs-Al3+ were − 6.49 eV and − 2.22 eV, respectively (Fig. 6g). The DFT results revealed that electrons could not transfer from the LUMO orbital of CN-CDs-Al3+ (−2.22 eV) to L-Lys (0.18 eV), which supported that the fluorescence decrease of CN-CDs-Al3+ was due to IFE rather than electron transfer process[2,22]. In summary, the fluorescence quenching of CN-CDs-Al3+ was due to the synergistic impact of static quenching and IFE.

To investigate the interaction between CN-CDs-Al3+ and D-Lys, UV–vis absorption spectrum and DFT was tested. Upon the addition of D-Lys, no obvious shift for the absorption peak was observed, which suggested that D-Lys did not strongly combine with CN-CDs-Al3+ (Fig. 6a). To further delve into the mechanism of chiral recognition, the possible interaction forces between CN-CDs-Al3+ and L-Lys or D-Lys were simulated with Gaussian 16. The results displayed that the Gibbs free energy between CN-CDs-Al3+ and L-Lys (−47.66 kj/mol) (Fig. 6h) was greater than that between CN-CDs-Al3+ and D-Lys (−44.09 kj/mol) due to stereochemical selectivity (Fig. 6i), indicating a stronger interactional force between CN-CDs-Al3+ and L-Lys. Therefore, CN-CDs-Al3+ system could achieve distinguishing chiral lysine[40,41].

L-Lysine is an essential amino acid for the growth and development of living organisms and is abundant in high-protein dairy products such as milk powder, pure milk, and yogurt. To evaluate the feasibility of CN-CDs for detecting Lys in actual samples, six dairy products were diluted with deionized water and subjected to fluorescence detection at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm. The initial concentrations of L-Lys in diluted dairy products were detected at firstly. As shown in Table 1 , the contents of L-Lys in the selected milk products were found to be in the range of 20.00–60.00 μM, which was consistent with previously reported literature[42]. Subsequently, 30.00, 35.00, and 45.00 μM of L-Lys were added to the diluted solutions to test the reliability and utility of the CDs sensors. The recovery rates of L-Lys in the six dairy products ranged from 94.83 % to 118.25 % with relative standard deviations (RSD) less than 1.93 %. Therefore, CN-CDs could be applied as flexible and efficient fluorescent sensor for the quantification of L-Lys in dairy products, offering a promising strategy for nutritional analysis and quality control in the food science and nutrition research communities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone application for the detection of Al3+ and l-lys
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the developing trend of on-site and real time detection methods, the CDs-loaded gel microsphere solid sensor was developed to portably and visually detect Al3+ and L-Lys. The images of gel microspheres were captured under 365 nm excitation by a smartphone. The RGB values were obtained using a color recognition selector APP to digitize the image data (Fig. 7 a-b). The fluorescence of the solid sensor shifted progressively from yellow-green to cyan-green as the Al3+ concentration increased within the 0–1.50 mM (Fig. 7c). As displayed in Fig. 7e, a linear correlation was established between the value of green (G) and Al3+ concentration (R2 = 0.999). Furthermore, with the rise in L-Lys concentration, the fluorescence intensity of the solid-state sensor diminished progressively (Fig. 7d). As illustrated in Fig. 7f, a good linear relationship R/G = 0.10CL-Lys + 0.02 (R2 = 0.999) was obtained between the G/R and L-Lys concentration from 0 to7.5 mM. The results indicated that the smartphone-supported sensing system facilitated visual and portable detection of Al3+ and L-Lys.

To further investigate the potential applications of CN-CDs in food field, the CCK-8 assay was employed to evaluate the cytotoxicity of CN-CDs on HeLa cells. The cells maintained over 85 % viability after 24 h of treatment with varying concentrations of CN-CDs (100–800 mg/mL) (Fig. S10). The cell viability of these CN-CDs was above 85 %, which was beneficial for their further application in the fields of food safety and biological detection. Subsequently, CN-CDs were utilized for multi-color cell imaging. HeLa cells were incubated with 300 mg/mL CN-CDs for 24 h, and multi-color fluorescence images of the cells was collected on inverted fluorescence microscopy. Subsequently, Al3+ was introduced into the cells at 100 mg/mL concentration. After 30 min incubation, a significant fluorescence enhancement was observed due to the occurrence of AIE of CN-CDs in the cells. A notable fluorescence quenching of CN-CDs-Al3+ was observed with the introduction of L-Lys into cells (Fig. 8 ), but the fluorescence decreased slightly upon the addition of D-Lys. Thus, the synthesized CN-CDs achieved the chiral distinction of L/D-Lys in cells under the assistance of Al3+.

In summary, a CDs-based fluorescent sensor had been developed for the highly selective and sensitive detection of Al3+ and lysine enantiomers. The CN-CDs exhibited stable and bright yellow-green fluorescence under 350 nm excitation. The fluorescence intensity and lifetime of CN-CDs were significantly enhanced under the induction of aluminum ions due to AIE derived from the formation of Al-O bonds through the coordination of CDs with Al3+. The fluorescence intensity of the CN-CDs-Al3+ system could be quenched by L-Lys through the static quenching and IFE. Therefore, the CDs-based sensor demonstrated excellent chiral recognition for L-Lys in the presence of Al3+ ions, with a LOD of 0.42 μM. It was noteworthy that the CDs-based sensing platform integrated with a smartphone had enabled real-time fluorescence detection and color analysis for Al3+ and L-Lys. According to the above results, the practicality of CDs-based fluorescence sensors was confirmed through the analysis of actual samples, providing new support for ensuring the safety of dairy food products.

Kaixin Hu: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Wanqing Ni: Software, Methodology. Chenzhao Wang: Software, Methodology. Kaihua Su: Visualization, Software, Methodology, Data curation. Xiangyu Yin: Software, Methodology, Data curation. Xuewei Song: Software, Data curation. Min Liu: Software. Guoying Sun: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Shanshan Wei: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.113945.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enantioselective Glutamic Acid Discrimination and Nanobiological Imaging by Chiral Fluorescent Silicon Nanoparticles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Han, M. Kou, K. Quan, J. Wang, H. Zhang, H. Ihara, M. Takafuji, H. Qiu, Enantioselective Glutamic Acid Discrimination and Nanobiological Imaging by Chiral Fluorescent Silicon Nanoparticles, Anal. Chem. 96 (2024) 2173–2182. doi: 10.1021/acs.analchem.3c05150.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: D. Guo, Preparation and application of achiral UiO-66-NH2 MOFs for enantioselective fluorescent detection of lysine enantiomers, 282 (2025) 126972–126980. doi: 10.1016/j.talanta.2024.126972.
